content.text:
TCP and UDP are both transport-layer protocols that sit on top of IP, but they make opposite tradeoffs between reliability and speed.

## TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)

**Connection-oriented and reliable.** It establishes a connection before sending data and guarantees delivery.

- **Handshake:** Sets up a connection via a 3-way handshake (SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK) before any data flows.
- **Reliable delivery:** Every segment is acknowledged; lost data is retransmitted.
- **Ordered:** Data arrives in the same order it was sent (reassembled using sequence numbers).
- **Flow & congestion control:** Adjusts sending rate to avoid overwhelming the receiver or the network.
- **Higher overhead:** Larger headers (20+ bytes) and more round trips.

## UDP (User Datagram Protocol)

**Connectionless and best-effort.** It just fires off packets ("datagrams") with no setup and no guarantees.

- **No handshake:** Send immediately, no connection state.
- **No delivery guarantee:** Packets can be lost, duplicated, or arrive out of order — the app must handle that if it cares.
- **No congestion/flow control** (by default).
- **Low overhead:** Small 8-byte header, minimal latency.

## Quick comparison

| | TCP | UDP |
|---|---|---|
| Connection | Yes (handshake) | No |
| Reliability | Guaranteed | Best-effort |
| Ordering | Preserved | Not guaranteed |
| Speed/latency | Slower, more overhead | Faster, lean |
| Header size | 20–60 bytes | 8 bytes |
| Use cases | Web (HTTP/1–2), email, file transfer, SSH | Video/voice calls, live streaming, gaming, DNS |

## The mental model

Use **TCP** when every byte must arrive correctly and in order (loading a web page, downloading a file). Use **UDP** when speed matters more than perfection and a little loss is acceptable (a dropped frame in a video call is better than a stalled one).

Worth noting: HTTP/3 runs over **QUIC**, which is built on UDP but adds reliability and ordering back in at the application layer — getting UDP's low latency while re-implementing the parts of TCP it wants.

## TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)

**Connection-oriented and reliable.** It establishes a connection before sending data and guarantees delivery.

- **Higher overhead:** Larger headers (20+ bytes) and more round trips.

**Connectionless and best-effort.** It just fires off packets ("datagrams") with no setup and no guarantees.

- **No handshake:** Send immediately, no connection state.

- **No congestion/flow control** (by default).
- **Low overhead:** Small 8-byte header, minimal latency.

| | TCP | UDP |
|---|---|---|
| Connection | Yes (handshake) | No |
| Reliability | Guaranteed | Best-effort |
| Ordering | Preserved | Not guaranteed |
| Speed/latency | Slower, more overhead | Faster, lean |
| Header size | 20–60 bytes | 8 bytes |

Use **TCP** when every byte must arrive correctly and in order (loading a web page, downloading a file). Use **UDP** when speed matters more than perfection and a little loss is acceptable (a dropped frame in a video call is better than a stalled one).
